{"paper_title": "Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont", "figure_id": "img_50", "figure_title": "Fig. 9. Changes in the ozone production regime over the 5 boxes denoted in Fig. 8.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont\figure_9.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["ozone production regime", "metropolitan areas", "Seattle", "Portland", "Los Angeles", "San Francisco", "Dallas", "Houston", "Chicago", "Detroit", "Pittsburgh", "New York"], "concise_summary": "Figure 9 displays the ozone production regime changes in five metropolitan areas in the U.S. for the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, using geospatial maps.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate shifts in ozone chemical regimes due to variations in NO2 and HCHO columns, highlighting the transition from NOx-saturated to transition or NOx-limited regimes in major cities.", "references": ["To examine the changes in the ozone chemical regime over major metropolitan cities more closely, we plotted the spatial distribution of the ozone production regime zoomed in over 5 boxes (Box A to Box E, denoted in Fig. 8) in Fig. 9, showing details of the selected metropolitan areas surrounding Seattle/Portland, Los Angeles/San Francisco, Dallas/Houston, Chicago, and Detroit/Pittsburgh/New York for the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017. In addition, Fig. 10 shows changes in  $\mathrm{HCHO} / \mathrm{NO}_2$  ratio at urban cores for the major 12 cities over the CONUS. The change of the ozone production regime varied during the study period. As a response to the reduction of  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns in urban regions, which was greater than that of HCHO columns (Fig. S8), the clear shifts of the ozone chemical regime from  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated to transition, or transition to  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -", "# Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the contiguous United States inferred by the inversion of  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and VOC emissions using satellite observation", "To investigate changes in the ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  chemical production regime over the contiguous United States (CONUS) with accurate knowledge of concentrations of its precursors, we applied an inverse modeling technique with Ozone Monitoring Instrument (OMI) tropospheric nitrogen dioxide  $(\mathrm{NO}_2)$  and total formaldehyde (HCHO) retrieval products in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which United States National Emission Inventory were based. The inclusion of dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions significantly account for the contribution of background sources in the free troposphere. Satellite-constrained nitrogen oxide  $(\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}})$  and non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs) emissions mitigate the discrepancy between satellite and modeled columns: the inversion suggested 2.33-2.84 (1.07-1.34) times higher  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  over the CONUS (over urban regions) and 0.28-0.81 times fewer NMVOCs emissions over the southeastern United States. The model-derived HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio shows gradual spatial changes in the  $\mathrm{O}_3$  production regime near urban cores relative to previously defined threshold values representing  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and VOC sensitive conditions. We also found apparent shifts from the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime to the transition regime (or the transition regime to the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited regime) over the major cities in the western United States. In contrast, rural areas, especially in the east-southeastern United States, exhibit a decreased HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio by  $-1.30 \pm 1.71$  with a reduction in HCHO column primarily driven by meteorology, becoming sensitive to VOC emissions. Results show that incorporating satellite observations into numerical modeling could help policymakers implement appropriate emission control policies for  $\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution.", "In this study, we applied an analytical inverse modeling technique with OMI  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO column retrievals and estimated top-down  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions over the contiguous U.S. (CONUS) in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, which were the base years that the U.S. NEI was formulated. From adjusted emissions, we examined changes in the ozone chemical regime over the CONUS throughout the study period.", "Before investigating changes in the ozone chemical production regime, we adjusted total  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions with the OMI  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO column retrievals found by the inverse modeling approaches that we addressed in Section 2.4 in order to acquire accurate knowledge of the concentrations of ozone precursors. It is worth noting that we assumed the uncertainty of bottom-up emissions as the main contributor to the discrepancy between modeled (with prior emissions, dynamic boundary conditions, and LNO emissions) and satellite-observed columns; the discrepancy, however, could also be explained by several other deficiencies in chemical processes (e.g., a lifetime of alkyl nitrate) and meteorology (e.g., wind fields).", "# 3.3. Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the CONUS", "threshold values by using the CMAQ model with posterior emission inventory based on the year 2011. Fig. 7 (a) represents the difference of averaged surface  $\mathrm{O_3}$  concentrations between the baseline CMAQ model and the CMAQ with perturbed emission (20% reductions in  $\mathrm{NO_x}$  and NMVOCs emissions) along with the corresponding modeled HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio for the revisit time of the OMI satellite (13:00-14:00 LST) since we adjusted emissions of ozone precursors based on OMI column retrievals. In addition, the revisit time of the OMI satellite is beneficial to analyze the ozone chemical regime when the ozone production is maximum in a day in conjunction with the smallest solar zenith angle and most profound boundary layer height (Jin et al., 2017). To clearly examine the difference of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  with perturbed emission, we considered model grids over polluted regions where the modeled tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column is greater than  $1\times 10^{15}$  molecules  $\mathrm{cm}^{-2}$  (Boersma et al., 2016; Choi et al., 2012; Schaub et al., 2006). As a response to  $\mathrm{NO_x}$  emission reduction, surface  $\mathrm{O_3}$  concentrations generally increased proportionally to the HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio. The increase was dominant for a high HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio. The change in surface ozone concentrations became moderate for the case of NMVOCs emission reduction (red color); still, noticeable changes in ozone concentration were found for a low HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio.", "Fig. 8 (a) shows the spatial distribution of the ozone chemical production regime (based on the averaged HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio) over the CONUS in the summer of 2011, while the results for 2014 and 2017 are plotted in Fig. S11. The entire CONUS is divided into three regimes according to the threshold values we defined earlier; model grids with NO $_2$  column density less than  $1 \times 10^{15}$  molecules  $\mathrm{cm}^{-2}$  are not considered and are denoted by the gray color. Several metropolitan cities were categorized as  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ -saturated and transition regimes, whereas the remaining rural areas were classified as  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ -limited regimes due to the nature of urban conditions with highly concentrated traffic density and amount of vehicle emissions. According to Fig. 8(b), showing the difference of HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio between 2011 and 2017, the column ratio in rural areas decreased by  $-1.30 \pm 1.71$ , mostly over the eastern and southeastern U.S. This regime modification toward the  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ -saturated condition could be explained by the increased  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions over the northeastern U.S. (Fig. S7), as well as the sustained decrease of the HCHO column (Fig. 3 (h-2)) as a response to meteorological change (Fig. S2).", "(b) Difference of HCHO/NO  ${}_{2}$  ratio between 2011 and 2017  
Fig. 8. (a) Spatial distribution of the ozone chemical production regime over the CONUS for the year 2011. (b) The difference between the  $\mathrm{HCHO / NO_2}$  ratios of 2011 and 2017. Gray areas represent tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column densities that are less than  $1\times 10^{15}$  molecules  $\mathrm{cm}^{-2}$ .", "limited were found partially over metropolitan areas (e.g., Seattle, Houston, and Pittsburgh) or completely even at urban cores (e.g., Houston, San Francisco, and Salt Lake City), especially from 2014 to 2017. By contrast, Los Angeles, New York, and Portland were the cities steadily remaining in the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime throughout the study period. Also, we observed the sudden expansions of  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated conditions at the urban cores of Houston (in 2014) and Detroit (in 2017). In general, most cities in the eastern U.S., except Pittsburgh, tended to stay in the same regime to which they were in 2011 or even came back to the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime compared to the cities in the western U.S. Many recent studies have reported the changes in ozone chemical regime over the major U.S. metropolitan areas toward  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited regime in general, but with a narrow range in the reported year when the regime change occurred, and cities still remained in  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited condition, which is related to the dataset, study period, and methodology of each study; Chang et al. (2016) showed that ozone formation in Boston, Pittsburgh, Philadelphia, and Washington has changed to  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited or transition regimes in 2007-2014, while New York City mostly stayed in the transition regime (2007-2009) and VOC-limited regime (2007-2009 and 2014). Jin et al. (2020) addressed that  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated condition is occurring only in Los Angeles, Chicago, and New York City by 2013-2016, and Koplitz et al. (2021) showed regime change toward  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited condition over most of the U.S., and a few cities such as New York City, Chicago, Minneapolis, San Francisco, and Los Angeles are still remained in  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime as in 2016.", "In this study, we examined changes in the ozone chemical regime over the CONUS in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which U.S. NEI data were based. To combine the benefits of both satellite retrievals and CTMs, we employed an inverse modeling technique with OMI tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column retrievals to estimate  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions. To avoid misalignment of the emission adjustment, we applied dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions, resulting in better representation of  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{O}_3$  concentration in the upper atmosphere, confirmed by comparisons with measurements from the DISCOVER-AQ campaigns and two ozonesonde sites.", "Compared to OMI observations, the model with the top-down estimated  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions showed remarkably better performance for simulating tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO columns and alleviated the nationwide underestimation of the  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column and the overestimation of the HCHO column appearing over the southeastern U. S. We then used the CMAQ model to determine the threshold values of the ozone production regime, which closely represented the ozone chemical conditions of this study:  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated (HCHO/  $\mathrm{NO}_2 < 1.6$ ), a transition  $(1.6 < \mathrm{HCHO} / \mathrm{NO}_2 < 2.6)$ , and  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited (HCHO/  $\mathrm{NO}_2 > 2.6$ ), which is slightly distinct from other studies by Chang et al. (2016), Choi et al. (2012), Duncan et al. (2010) and Jin et al. (2017). This is because the threshold value of the HCHO/  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  ratio represents the unique ozone chemistry of the CTM depending upon several influencing factors such as study domain, chemical mechanism, and meteorological condition; therefore, the applicability of the threshold value in other", "direct-sun DOAS technique: Intercomparisons and application to OMI validation. source_args.query_intent: narrative or storytelling visualizations of air‑pollution data using platforms like Observable HQ or Shiny narrative dashboards

evaluation.extracted_insight: 图 9 通过地理空间可视化展示了 2011、2014、2017 年美国五大城市群的臭氧生成机制变化，显示 NOx‑饱和向过渡或 NOx‑受限 regime 的转变，主要受 NO2 与 HCHO 列柱比值变化驱动。
evaluation.reason: 提供了直观的区域臭氧化学机制演变图，能够帮助识别哪些城市的臭氧前体控制策略需要调整，是后续模型校准和政策评估的重要依据。

content.text: Figure 3 illustrates per capita emissions and emissions per GDP for various substances across different country income groups in 2010 using box plots, highlighting percentile ranges and specific regions. The figure provides a comparative analysis of pollution emissions by income group and GDP contribution, indicating regional disparities and emphasizing the significance of income levels on emission patterns.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "HTAP_v2.2_ a mosaic of regional and global emissio", "figure_id": "img_139", "figure_title": "Figure 3. content.text: Vertical profiles show particle concentrations upwind and downwind of a motorway, revealing height-dependent differences and correlations with NOx levels. The figure illustrates how particle concentrations vary with height and time, confirming correlations with NOx and revealing impacts of emissions on air quality.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Comparison of measured and model-calculated real-w", "figure_id": "img_56", "figure_title": "Fig. 5. Upwind the motorway, no height-dependent difference in the particle number and surface area concentration was observed. On the downwind side, substantially higher concentrations were measured within the plume from near the ground up to  $20\\mathrm{m}$  height than above the plume (Fig. 5). This height dependency was found for particles with diameter  $< 300\\mathrm{nm}$ , which are soot particles and nucleation mode particles formed by condensation of exhaust gases in most cases and for coarse particles (diameter  $>1\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ), originating from tyre and brake abrasion and resuspension of soil dust. Between 300 and  $700\\mathrm{nm}$  particle size, no height dependence was found, because motor vehicles emit only a few particles in this size range.", "After adjusting the instruments for their different time lags, the vertical distribution of the particle number concentrations showed a similar behaviour as the  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  concentrations: No height dependence on the upwind side and a continuous decrease with increasing height from 5 to  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above the earth's surface on the downwind side was found. Consequently, the results in this study confirmed the correlation between particles and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  values that had also been observed in former case studies. A linear relation between the particle surface area (SA) and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  with correlation coefficients of  $R^2 = 0.96$  and 0.89 for 2 downwind cases was found. At around  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above ground, the background value of SA was attained. By subtracting the background value from the concentrations measured in the near-surface air layer, direct emissions of the road were obtained, even if contemporaneous upwind and downwind measurements were missing. Together with the parallel wind measurements, mean absolute emission factors (EF) were determined. The PM emissions in the range between  $30\\mathrm{nm}$  and  $10\\mu \\mathrm{m}$  from all vehicles passing the site were found to be  $1.80\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ . Separation into the categories of LDV led to  $1.22\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.03\\mathrm{cm}^3\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  aerosol volume, while the values of HDV were  $7.79\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.41\\mathrm{cm}^3\\mathrm{km}^{-1}$  veh $^{-1}$  particle volume.", "Fig. 4. args.query_intent: mobile‑app interactive visualizations for air‑pollution data (Flutter, React Native, Android, iOS)

orchestrator_plan.reason: 移动端交互可实现实时、地点感知的空气质量展示，现有策略均聚焦于桌面/Web，可补足新维度。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 这个策略的出发点是希望找到面向移动端（Flutter、React Native、Android、iOS）的交互式空气质量可视化实现方案，以便在手机应用中实时、位置感知地展示污染数据。但实际检索到的十条记录几乎全部是关于空气污染本身的原始数据或传统的静态图表：有中国气象与空气质量平台的数据来源说明（结果 1、2），展示亚洲排放网格图、隧道内浓度剖面、道路两侧垂直粒子分布以及 VOC 时序箱线图等空间或时间序列图形（结果 3‑5、8、10），还有一张列出 2013‑2020 年中国大气治理政策的概览图（结果 9）。其中两条仅剩片段或标题，被标记为噪声、无实际信息（结果 6、7），其余则为重复内容。总体来看，检索结果围绕空气污染数据集、传统科研图示和政策汇编展开，缺乏任何针对 Flutter、React Native 或其他移动平台的交互式可视化实现细节、代码示例或框架指导。因而该策略虽意在补足移动端可视化的维度，却实际上提供的主要是数据来源与已有的静态可视化示例，而未直接解决移动端交互可视化的具体实现问题。

content.text: Dependent variables in this study were used as indicators measuring air pollution, particularly AQI,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO, and  $\mathrm{NO}_2$ . Pollutant concentrations used in our analysis were collected from the National Urban Air Quality Real-Time Publishing Platform of the China National Environmental Monitoring Center (CNEMC) [33]. Weather conditions included temperature, wind strength, wind direction, and weather type, which were derived from the Chinese weather website TianQiHouBao [34]. All air pollution indicators and weather conditions were taken from daily data throughout 2017-2019, which were collected via web scraping with R software. Macroeconomic data (i.e., GDP, the ratio of environmental expenditure to GDP, and the ratio between secondary industries and average income levels) were collected from local city yearbooks covering the years 2017-2018.

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. content.text: Vertical profiles show particle concentrations upwind and downwind of a motorway, revealing height-dependent differences and correlations with NOx levels. The figure illustrates how particle concentrations vary with height and time, confirming correlations with NOx and revealing impacts of emissions on air quality.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Comparison of measured and model-calculated real-w", "figure_id": "img_56", "figure_title": "Fig. 5. Upwind the motorway, no height-dependent difference in the particle number and surface area concentration was observed. On the downwind side, substantially higher concentrations were measured within the plume from near the ground up to  $20\\mathrm{m}$  height than above the plume (Fig. 5). This height dependency was found for particles with diameter  $< 300\\mathrm{nm}$ , which are soot particles and nucleation mode particles formed by condensation of exhaust gases in most cases and for coarse particles (diameter  $>1\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ), originating from tyre and brake abrasion and resuspension of soil dust. Between 300 and  $700\\mathrm{nm}$  particle size, no height dependence was found, because motor vehicles emit only a few particles in this size range.", "After adjusting the instruments for their different time lags, the vertical distribution of the particle number concentrations showed a similar behaviour as the  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  concentrations: No height dependence on the upwind side and a continuous decrease with increasing height from 5 to  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above the earth's surface on the downwind side was found. Consequently, the results in this study confirmed the correlation between particles and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  values that had also been observed in former case studies. A linear relation between the particle surface area (SA) and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  with correlation coefficients of  $R^2 = 0.96$  and 0.89 for 2 downwind cases was found. At around  $30\\mathrm{m}$  above ground, the background value of SA was attained. By subtracting the background value from the concentrations measured in the near-surface air layer, direct emissions of the road were obtained, even if contemporaneous upwind and downwind measurements were missing. Together with the parallel wind measurements, mean absolute emission factors (EF) were determined. The PM emissions in the range between  $30\\mathrm{nm}$  and  $10\\mu \\mathrm{m}$  from all vehicles passing the site were found to be  $1.80\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ . Separation into the categories of LDV led to  $1.22\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.03\\mathrm{cm}^3\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  aerosol volume, while the values of HDV were  $7.79\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.41\\mathrm{cm}^3\\mathrm{km}^{-1}$  veh $^{-1}$  particle volume.", "Fig. 4. Vertical profiles of mean particle size distributions measured by SMPS instruments at three different heights on the upwind side of the motorway (13 May 2001, midnight) and on the downwind side (14 May 2001, morning rush hour and noon)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

orchestrator_plan.reason: 高维度污染特征的聚类与模式可视化在已有文献中缺失，能够揭示隐含来源与时空关联。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 这个策略的出发点是希望通过机器学习的降维可视化手段（如 t‑SNE、UMAP、PCA）来呈现高维空气污染数据的空间‑时空模式，从而帮助发现潜在的污染源和关联。然而检索到的文献主要围绕空气质量的深度学习预测、空间格点排放数据以及时序‑空间特征提取展开。几篇结果（比如 chunk_000493 和 chunk_004469）展示了 CNN‑LSTM 等网络如何捕捉非线性时空相关性，为后续特征抽取提供了思路；而 chunk_004609、 chunk_003109、 chunk_002660 以及 chunk_002790 则提供了高分辨率的格点排放地图（MIX、HTAP_v2.2 等），这些格点数据是进行降维可视化的原始高维输入的可靠来源；此外， chunk_001486 介绍了利用 CEMS 实时监测转化为日尺度排放库的框架，为构建时间维度丰富的特征矩阵提供了数据支撑。总体来看，检索结果虽未直接给出降维可视化实例，却围绕高质量的时空排放数据集和深度学习特征提取模型，提供了实现该可视化目标的关键数据来源和方法线索。

content.text: Compared with traditional techniques for forecasting air pollution, namely empirical approaches and simple statistical models, deep learning approaches can handle complex and non-linear relationships among air quality variables which allow them to better capture the pollution episode forecasts. Therefore, deep learning has been identified as a vital tool for air quality modelling and forecasting, which helps in recognizing pollution hot spots (Schmidhuber, 2015; Ong et al., 2016; Li et al., 2017).

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "Guan, D., Liu, Z., Geng, Y., Lindner, S., and Hubacek, K.: The gigatonne gap in China's carbon dioxide inventories, Nature Clim. source_args.query_intent: machine‑learning dimensionality‑reduction visualizations (t‑SNE, UMAP, PCA) of high‑dimensional air‑pollution datasets

content.text: The figure illustrates the CNN-LSTM architecture used for air quality prediction, showcasing the model's encoder-decoder structure and workflow for feature extraction and forecasting. The tailored CNN-LSTM model is designed to effectively capture temporal-spatial correlations in air quality data, reducing training issues and improving prediction accuracy.

content.text: To further analyze the temporal and spatial variation features of air quality in 31 provincial capital cities from 2013 to 2020, the spatial autocorrelation analysis method is applied. ArcGIS 10.6 and GeoDa software is used for the calculation and analytic process.

content.text: A harmonized global, gridded, air pollution emission data set has been compiled with officially reported, gridded inventories at the national scale, to the extent possible and complemented with science-based inventories for regions and sectors where nationally reported data were not available.

content.text: In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.

evaluation.extracted_insight: MIX 排放清单以 0.25°×0.25° 的分辨率提供 10 类气体与气溶胶的格点排放数据，并融合了中国的 CPED、 日本的 JEI‑DB 与 OPRF 等高分辨率本地数据，提升了空间模式的准确性。
evaluation.reason: 该清单为高分辨率排放分布提供了可靠数据来源，可直接用于评估政策或模型输入，对研究的实证部分具有重要价值。

content.text: This paper describes the HTAP global air pollutant reference emission inventory for 2010, which is composed of latest available data from regional inventory compilers. It assures a consistent input for both regional and global modelling as required by the HTAP modelling exercise. The HTAP_v2.2 emission database makes use of consolidated estimates of official and latest available regional information with air pollutant grid maps from US EPA and Environment Canada for North America, EMEP-TNO for Europe, MIX for Asia, and the EDGARv4.3 database for the rest of the world. The mosaic of grid maps provides comprehensive local information on the emission of air pollutants, because it results from the collection of point sources and national emission grid maps at  $0.1^{\circ}$  (for some regions  $0.25^{\circ}$ ) resolution. Even though the HTAP_v2.2 data set is not a self-consistent bottom-up database with activity data of consistent international statistics, with harmonized emission factors, and with global sets of spatial proxy data, it provides a unique set of emission grid maps with global coverage and high spatial resolution, including in particular important point sources. The compilation of implied emission factors and per capita emissions for the different world regions using multiple sources provides the regional and national emission inventory compilers with a valuable asset for comparison with their own data for cross checking and analysis which may lead to identification of future improvement options.

content.text: Despite the limitations, our study offers a framework to apply emission measurements from CEMS to improving the temporal representation of emission inventories. Insight obtained from this work could be extended to other sectors (e.g., cement, iron, and steel). In the future, we expect to develop a multisector dataset at the daily scale via data fusion to facilitate atmospheric research and clean air policy formation in China.

evaluation.reason: Directly addresses the need for high‑temporal‑resolution emission data and points to concrete next steps (multisector daily dataset), which is valuable for deep‑dive analysis of daily emission patterns.

args.query_intent: integrated source‑apportionment visualizations combining Sankey/alluvial diagrams with GIS spatial maps

orchestrator_plan.reason: 将源流关系图与空间分布结合，提供更直观的来源解析，可视化深度与广度均未被覆盖。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 这个策略的目标是抓取能够同时展示源‑流关系（如 Sankey 或河流图）和空间分布（GIS‑式地图）的综合可视化，以便更直观地进行排放来源归属分析。实际检索到的 10 条记录大多以图形形式呈现地理范围和排放数据：包括 CONUS 与中国等研究域的空间概览、基于 Landsat 8 的区域边界图、以及展示亚洲多国 MIX 排放清单的区域分布图和 2010 年各污染物 sector‑specific 排放总量的饼图叠加世界地图。还有高分辨率的气体与气溶胶网格图，直观呈现了功能区的排放强度和空间模式。虽然部分条目仅包含作者机构或基金声明，被标记为剔除，但保留下来的图形资料为后续把源‑流关系关联到空间分布提供了关键的地理背景和定量排放信息，为实现“源‑流‑空间”一体化的可视化奠定了重要线索。

Our study domain covering the CONUS utilizes  $12\\mathrm{km}$  grid spacing with  $459\\times 299$  grid points (shown in Fig. 1), while the vertical extent between the surface and  $100\\mathrm{hPa}$  is discretized with 27 layers of variable thickness. We use carbon bond (CB6r3) for the gas phase and AERO6 for the aerosol chemical mechanism. We obtained the chemical initial and boundary conditions from the CMAQ model with the in-line dust model covering the entire Northern Hemisphere (HCMAQ) (Mathur et al., 2017), providing dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions in a consistent physical configuration. Details of the HCMAQ simulation and its emission data can be found in Jung et al. (2021) and Vukovich et al. (2018). Since the HCMAQ simulation was based on the year 2016, we applied seasonal scaling for gaseous species based on the comparison", "Using the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model version 4.0, we estimated meteorological fields over the U.S.  $12\\mathrm{km}$  modeling domain (shown in Fig. 1) with the initial and boundary conditions from the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR), which has a  $32\\mathrm{km}$  horizontal resolution and  $3\\mathrm{h}$  of temporal resolution. Detailed configurations of both the CMAQ and WRF models can be found in Table 1. In an effort to enhance model performance in simulating meteorological fields, we applied indirect soil moisture and temperature nudging technique (Pleim and Gilliam, 2009; Pleim and Xiu, 2003) together with a four-", "The variability of satellite-observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns reflects changes in bottom-up emissions and the contribution of background sources in the upper troposphere (Silvern et al., 2019). In Fig. 3, we plotted seasonal averaged changes in observed and simulated tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities in four regions: domainwide (i.e., CONUS), urban and rural regions, and southeastern (SE) areas. We defined an urban area based on the U.S. census-based surrogate, where population density is at least 1000 people per square mile, denoted in red in Fig. 1; and we defined a rural region, designated by gray in Fig. 1, as more than  $20\\mathrm{km}$  away from both urban regions and power plants with a capacity of 1 MW or more, based on the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The southeastern (SE) area (a blue rectangle shown in Fig. 1) denotes an area with dominant lightning-induced (to the total  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions) and biogenic emissions (to the total NMVOCs emissions) over the CONUS. In SE, NMVOCs emissions from biogenic sources are dominant, so we excluded the SE area when we analyzed modeled and observed HCHO columns over urban and rural regions in Fig. 3.", "Fig. 3. The long-term trend in summertime averaged tropospheric (a-1 to h-1)  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and (a-2 to h-2) HCHO columns observed by the OMI satellite (2011-2018) and simulated by the CMAQ model (2011, 2014, and 2017), and their relative changes in the CONUS and urban, rural, and southeastern U.S. regions (shown in Fig. 1). CMAQ simulations were conducted with the prior emission and three background setups (with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and lightning emissions (LNO), with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions, and with profile lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}
