{"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_165", "figure_title": "Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "Guan, D., Liu, Z., Geng, Y., Lindner, S., and Hubacek, K.: The gigatonne gap in China's carbon dioxide inventories, Nature Clim. content.text: The figure displays the location and setup of air pollution monitoring sites within East Yan'an Road Tunnel, Shanghai. It includes schematic representations of the tunnel and monitoring locations. The schematic provides spatial context for the air quality monitoring study, highlighting the strategic placement of sensors to capture key pollutants from vehicle emissions in a high-traffic urban tunnel.

It includes schematic representations of the tunnel and monitoring locations.", "inferred_insight": "The schematic provides spatial context for the air quality monitoring study, highlighting the strategic placement of sensors to capture key pollutants from vehicle emissions in a high-traffic urban tunnel.", "references": ["A tunnel in the city center of Shanghai $(31.24^{\\circ}\\mathrm{N}, 121.50^{\\circ}\\mathrm{E})$ was selected to conduct air pollution monitoring. The tunnel is called East Yan'an Road tunnel, is an underwater tunnel crossing the Huangpu River in Shanghai. It has two bores with two lanes for each direction. The total length is $2,107\\mathrm{m}$ ; cross-sectional area is $33.75\\mathrm{m}^2$ . To continuously monitor major air pollutants from vehicle exhaust, we installed two integrated air pollution monitoring units at $150\\mathrm{m}$ away from the inlet and outlet of the tunnel, respectively. The height of the unit was about $1.2\\mathrm{m}$ . Fig. 2 shows the location of the tunnel and the monitoring sites. There were 4 sensors which could separately monitor NO, $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ , CO, and $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ concentrations in each unit. NO, $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ , and CO were detected using electrochemical method. Their detection limits were $0.01\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , $0.01\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , and $0.15\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , respectively. $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ was detected by light scattering method. Its resolution was $0.1\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . The monitoring data was continuously transmitted to a remote database through wireless network. The system was built in July 2016 and has been operated by now. To ensure the reliability of monitoring", "Vehicle flow and average speed in the tunnel were detected by a buried induction coil and transmitted to a data platform in real time. The fleet compositions were recognized by automatic acquisition of vehicle license plate information through a high-definition video in the tunnel. Fig. 4 shows the average traffic flow and fleet composition in each hour of weekday and weekend. The data between August and November of 2016 were used in this study. Daily average vehicle flow and speed were $34,818 \\pm 2,214 \\mathrm{veh} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ and $33.47 \\pm 7.95 \\mathrm{km} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ on weekday and were $30,818 \\pm 1,848 \\mathrm{veh} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ and $33.86 \\pm 8.83 \\mathrm{km} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ on weekend. Vehicle flows in peak hour were $2,173 \\pm 161$ and $1,952 \\pm 68 \\mathrm{veh} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ on weekday and weekend, respectively. East Yan'an Road tunnel was located in the city center of Shanghai, where heavy-duty diesel vehicles (HDDVs) were restricted to access except for some medium-duty diesel vehicles (MDDVs). Therefore, fleet in the tunnel was mainly comprised of light-duty gasoline vehicles, which accounted for $93.7 \\pm 2.2\\%$ and $94.6 \\pm 1.9\\%$ of the total on weekday and weekend, respectively. The", "tunnel in Taiwan in 2006. The CO, $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ EFs were $0.91 \\pm 0.47 - 1.47 \\pm 0.63$ , $0.15 \\pm 0.07 - 0.33 \\pm 0.17\\mathrm{gkm}^{-1} \\cdot \\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ , and $2 \\pm 2 - 4 \\pm 3\\mathrm{mgkm}^{-1} \\cdot \\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ , respectively. Their results were the lowest compared with other tunnel studies in China mainly because Taiwan implemented Tier 1 and Tier 2 emission standards in 1999 and 2008, earlier than the mainland of China. Liu et al. (2015a,b) and Deng et al. (2015) measured CO and $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ EFs in East Yan'an Road tunnel in 2012. CO EF decreased by about $30\\%$ compared with our result in 2016. $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ EF also declined if we considered $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ was similar to $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ for vehicle exhaust. The enforcement of vehicle emission control strategies should be the main reason for the decrease. Between 2012 and 2016, Shanghai implemented the emission standards of LDGVs from Euro 4 to Euro 5, eliminated Euro 0 gasoline vehicles from the whole city, and restricted Euro 1 gasoline vehicles in urban area. CO and $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ EFs in Yingpan Road tunnel were $0.75 \\pm 0.56 - 6.05 \\pm 5.94$ and $0.12 \\pm 0.02 - 0.82 \\pm 0.76\\mathrm{gkm}^{-1} \\cdot \\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ (Deng et al., 2015). Our results were relatively lower than the average value of Yingpan Road tunnel. The fractions of gasoline and diesel vehicles have influences on EFs in the tunnels. CO, $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ EFs in Zhujiang tunnel were much higher than those in our study since there was higher proportion of diesel vehicles (Liu et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2015b). Correspondingly, KXL tunnel had lower $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ EF due to its high proportion of gasoline vehicles (Cui et al., 2016).", "

args.query_intent: Vega‑Lite declarative specifications for web visualizations of air pollution datasets

orchestrator_plan.reason: Vega‑Lite 用 JSON 描述图表，易于嵌入网页并实现交互，可快速生成散点图、热图、层叠图等，提供与 D3 不同的低代码方案。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 这个策略的出发点是寻找能够直接用于 Vega‑Lite 的声明式可视化规范，以便在网页上快速呈现空气污染数据的散点图、热图或层叠图等交互式图形。然而检索结果主要围绕空气污染排放数据库及其已有的可视化实例展开，而非具体的 Vega‑Lite JSON 代码。出现的内容包括 HTAP 和全球分辨率排放清单的网格映射、亚洲地区高分辨率排放图、VOC 与烷烃的排放因子剖面、隧道内多污染物浓度随距离变化的趋势图、基于发动机负荷的有机碳排放因子对比图、以及按人均和 GDP 分组的排放量箱线图等。这些材料提供了丰富的原始排放数据、空间分布图和多变量可视化示例，能够为后续手工转换为 Vega‑Lite 规范提供数据来源和设计灵感，尽管当前并未直接命中低代码可视化的技术实现细节。

content.text: The resulting data set, named HTAP_v2.2, is a compilation of annual and monthly grid maps of anthropogenic air pollution emissions (with a  $0.1^{\circ} \times 0.1^{\circ}$  grid resolution). It contains region-specific information on human activity (concerning intensity and geospatial distribution) and on fuel, technology- and process-dependent emission factors and end-of-pipe abatement, but it is not as consistent as a globally consistent emission inventory using international statistics and global geospatial distributions. With the perspective of being used in chemical transport models, this inventory includes the atmospheric gaseous pollutants  $\mathrm{(SO_2, NO_x, CO, NMVOC^4, NH_3)}$  and particulate matter with carbonaceous speciation  $\mathrm{(PM_{10}, PM_{2.5}, BC}$  and OC)5.

content.text: A harmonized global, gridded, air pollution emission data set has been compiled with officially reported, gridded inventories at the national scale, to the extent possible and complemented with science-based inventories for regions and sectors where nationally reported data were not available.

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

metadata.metadata:
{"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_165", "figure_title": "Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. content.text: The figure presents emission factor profiles, separating VOCs and alkanes. The emissions of all PM components are modeled based on the variations of SFOC and instantaneous power, and in addition the emission factors of sulphate and associated water are dependent on the fuel sulphur content."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

args.query_intent: CARTO Builder tile‑based visualizations for large‑scale air quality point data

orchestrator_plan.reason: CARTO 的云平台支持海量传感器点数据的瓦片渲染与动态过滤，适合展示移动监测网络数据，现有策略未覆盖此商业 GIS 可视化平台。
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 这个策略的目标是寻找能够在 CARTO Builder 上通过瓦片渲染和动态过滤展示海量空气质量点数据的技术或案例，以弥补现有检索中缺少对这一商业 GIS 可视化平台的覆盖。实际检索得到的十条记录几乎全部是传统的空气质量空间可视化示例：包括基于 MIX 亚洲排放清单的气体和气溶胶网格图、展示 AQI、PM2.5、SO₂、NO₂ 分布的柱状图、美国大陆（CONUS）研究域的空间概览图、使用 ArcGIS 10.6 与 GeoDa 进行空间自相关分析的省会城市时空变化、隧道内多点监测的浓度剖面示意以及监测站点布设示意图等。内容整体围绕“地图/网格图、柱状图、空间分析和传感器布点”这些常规的空气质量可视化手段展开，未出现关于 CARTO Builder 瓦片渲染、动态过滤或其 API/案例的直接信息。唯一的潜在线索是这些示例都在说明大范围点数据的空间分布与可视化方法，暗示可以在 CARTO 平台上复用类似的网格或瓦片图层来实现对海量监测点的交互式展示，但需要进一步寻找专门针对 CARTO Builder 的实现细节或文档。

content.text: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

{"paper_title": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento", "figure_id": "img_165", "figure_title": "Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_12.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MIX Asian emission inventory", "gaseous species", "aerosol species", "2010", "CPED", "JEI-DB", "OPRF"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.", "inferred_insight": "The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.", "references": ["In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. content.text: The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions. The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden", "figure_id": "img_71", "figure_title": "Fig. 4. Estimated coefficients (date) prior to and following the start time of heating.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\\figure_4.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "PM2.5", "SO2", "NO2", "Eastern region", "Western region", "WCHP project"], "concise_summary": "The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.", "references": ["As Fig. 4 shows, the 15 day estimator prior to and following the start of heating varied around a value of zero (0) devoid of a clear trend, and most coefficients were not statistically significant. This result indicated a similarity between pilot cities and non-pilot cities regarding AQI. Despite an abnormal increase 4-6 days following the start time of heating, such differences decreased to a negative value (as seen 2 days prior to the start time of heating), and this trend maintained for the ensuing two weeks. These results were consistent with the hypothesis that the WCHP project generated differential trends in pollutant concentrations between pilot and non-pilot cities. It is noteworthy that the period prior to the start time of heating was implemented and continued for 3-4 days in some cities prior to the formal start time of heating, which may have resulted in the early decrease. Additionally, we also estimated the Heatperiod coefficient 30 days following the start time of heating, which also indicated that effects of the WCHP project existed following the start time of heating.", "where  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  is the set of dummy variables, which represents whether the  $i$ -th city is in a heating period at  $n-1$ -day.  $L_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days prior to the start time of heating, and  $E_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days following the start time of heating. With the omitted time category being the start time of heating,  $L_n$  captures the differences in pollutant indicators between pilot and non-pilot cities relative to differences just prior to the launch of the policy. Here we selected AQI as the air pollutant proxy. Under such specifications, if the signs of the estimated  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  coefficients differ, it can be assumed that changes in pollutant concentrations are not significant.", "As a second robustness check, we constructed a counterfactual test to check whether our results could be driven by other unobserved factors. In doing so, we selected a different date during the non-heating period (May, June, July, August, September, and October) in lieu of the real start date of heating, specifying the interaction term between the treatment variable and the pseudoheating start time variable as Pseudo-HCP. The WCHP project can only take effect during the heating period, while the pseudo-HCP coefficient can reflect the impact of the WCHP project during the non-heating period. If estimated coefficients of pseudo-WCHP are significant and have the same sign as of the true WCHP shown in Table 4, we can conclude that air quality improvements in pilot cities could not be mainly attributed to the WCHP project, and the robustness of the DID model would be inadequate. In this case, improvements may be related to other pollution control policies, such as production restrictions and limits to the number of private vehicle allowed on roads.", "According to the regressions shown in Table 5, only when the start time of heating was specified in October, coefficients of pseudo-WCHP were significantly negative at a  $1\\%$  level, while other estimation results were not significant or had a positive sign. Compared to Table 4, these conflicting results failed to reject the validity of the \"WHCD\" policy. That is to say, the effect of the WCHP project was evident. In fact, we specified the heating start time on the 15th of November in consideration of the \"official\" start time of central heating while ignoring differences caused by scattered coal combustion. In October, some rural areas in the pilot and non-pilot cities began to burn scattered coal for heating, which could cause an evident increase in pollution emissions [47,48]."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville). Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.

metadata.metadata: {"paper_title": "Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont", "figure_id": "img_42", "figure_title": "Fig. 1. Map of the study domain and definition of urban (red) and rural (gray) regions over the CONUS. The yellow triangle symbols represent the location of ozonesonde stations (BLD: Boulder, HVA: Huntsville). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont\\figure_1.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup", "Describe Data Distribution"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["CONUS", "ozonesonde stations", "CMAQ", "WRF", "urban regions", "rural regions", "Boulder", "Huntsville"], "concise_summary": "The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville).", "inferred_insight": "Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.", "references": ["To compute both atmospheric concentrations of gaseous and particulate pollutants and first-order sensitivity coefficients, required for conducting inverse modeling, we use the Community Multiscale Air Quality model configured with the decoupled direct method in three dimensions (CMAQ DDM-3D) based on CMAQ model version 5.2 (Cohan et al., 2005; Napelenok et al., 2006), developed and released by the EPA. Our study domain covering the CONUS utilizes  $12\\mathrm{km}$  grid spacing with  $459\\times 299$  grid points (shown in Fig. 1), while the vertical extent between the surface and  $100\\mathrm{hPa}$  is discretized with 27 layers of variable thickness. We use carbon bond (CB6r3) for the gas phase and AERO6 for the aerosol chemical mechanism. We obtained the chemical initial and boundary conditions from the CMAQ model with the in-line dust model covering the entire Northern Hemisphere (HCMAQ) (Mathur et al., 2017), providing dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions in a consistent physical configuration. Details of the HCMAQ simulation and its emission data can be found in Jung et al. (2021) and Vukovich et al. (2018). Since the HCMAQ simulation was based on the year 2016, we applied seasonal scaling for gaseous species based on the comparison", "Using the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model version 4.0, we estimated meteorological fields over the U.S.  $12\\mathrm{km}$  modeling domain (shown in Fig. 1) with the initial and boundary conditions from the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR), which has a  $32\\mathrm{km}$  horizontal resolution and  $3\\mathrm{h}$  of temporal resolution. Detailed configurations of both the CMAQ and WRF models can be found in Table 1. In an effort to enhance model performance in simulating meteorological fields, we applied indirect soil moisture and temperature nudging technique (Pleim and Gilliam, 2009; Pleim and Xiu, 2003) together with a four-", "The variability of satellite-observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns reflects changes in bottom-up emissions and the contribution of background sources in the upper troposphere (Silvern et al., 2019). In Fig. 3, we plotted seasonal averaged changes in observed and simulated tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities in four regions: domainwide (i.e., CONUS), urban and rural regions, and southeastern (SE) areas. We defined an urban area based on the U.S. census-based surrogate, where population density is at least 1000 people per square mile, denoted in red in Fig. 1; and we defined a rural region, designated by gray in Fig. 1, as more than  $20\\mathrm{km}$  away from both urban regions and power plants with a capacity of 1 MW or more, based on the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The southeastern (SE) area (a blue rectangle shown in Fig. 1) denotes an area with dominant lightning-induced (to the total  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions) and biogenic emissions (to the total NMVOCs emissions) over the CONUS. In SE, NMVOCs emissions from biogenic sources are dominant, so we excluded the SE area when we analyzed modeled and observed HCHO columns over urban and rural regions in Fig. 3.", "Fig. 3. The long-term trend in summertime averaged tropospheric (a-1 to h-1)  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and (a-2 to h-2) HCHO columns observed by the OMI satellite (2011-2018) and simulated by the CMAQ model (2011, 2014, and 2017), and their relative changes in the CONUS and urban, rural, and southeastern U.S. regions (shown in Fig. 1). CMAQ simulations were conducted with the prior emission and three background setups (with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and lightning emissions (LNO), with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions, and with profile lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions)."], "chunk_type": "figure"}

content.text: hance urban emissions and improve the model performance. When further applying industrial gross domestic product (IGDP) values for the industrial sector, modeled  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns could better capture pollution hotspots in urban areas and exhibit the best performance of the six cases compared to satellite-based  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns (slope  $= 1.01$  and  $R^2 = 0.85$ ). This analysis provides a framework for information from satellite observations to inform bottom-up inventory development. In the future, more effort should be devoted to the representation of spatial proxies to improve spatial patterns in bottom-up emission inventories.

evaluation.extracted_insight: Applying industrial gross domestic product (IGDP) as a spatial proxy improves the agreement between modeled NO₂ vertical columns and satellite observations, achieving a slope of 1.01 and R² = 0.85, indicating that IGDP captures urban pollution hotspots effectively.
evaluation.reason: Provides quantitative validation of a spatial proxy (IGDP) for bottom‑up emission inventories, with clear performance metrics and a direct link to satellite‑based verification, making it a high‑value evidence for inventory improvement.

content.text: The figure displays the location and setup of air pollution monitoring sites within East Yan'an Road Tunnel, Shanghai. It includes schematic representations of the tunnel and monitoring locations. The schematic provides spatial context for the air quality monitoring study, highlighting the strategic placement of sensors to capture key pollutants from vehicle emissions in a high-traffic urban tunnel.

metadata.metadata:
{"paper_title": "Evaluation of emission factors for light-duty gaso", "figure_id": "img_95", "figure_title": "Fig. 2. Schematic diagram of the monitoring site in the East Yan'an Road Tunnel, Shanghai.", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Evaluation of emission factors for light-duty gaso\\figure_2.jpg", "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Diagrams & Schematics", "sub_types": ["Conceptual Diagram"]}, "key_entities": ["East Yan'an Road Tunnel", "Shanghai", "Huangpu River", "air pollution monitoring", "vehicle exhaust", "sensors", "NO", "NO2", "CO", "PM2.5"], "concise_summary": "The figure displays the location and setup of air pollution monitoring sites within East Yan'an Road Tunnel, Shanghai. It includes schematic representations of the tunnel and monitoring locations.", "inferred_insight": "The schematic provides spatial context for the air quality monitoring study, highlighting the strategic placement of sensors to capture key pollutants from vehicle emissions in a high-traffic urban tunnel.", "references": ["A tunnel in the city center of Shanghai $(31.24^{\\circ}\\mathrm{N}, 121.50^{\\circ}\\mathrm{E})$ was selected to conduct air pollution monitoring. The tunnel is called East Yan'an Road tunnel, is an underwater tunnel crossing the Huangpu River in Shanghai. It has two bores with two lanes for each direction. The total length is $2,107\\mathrm{m}$ ; cross-sectional area is $33.75\\mathrm{m}^2$ . To continuously monitor major air pollutants from vehicle exhaust, we installed two integrated air pollution monitoring units at $150\\mathrm{m}$ away from the inlet and outlet of the tunnel, respectively. The height of the unit was about $1.2\\mathrm{m}$ . Fig. 2 shows the location of the tunnel and the monitoring sites. There were 4 sensors which could separately monitor NO, $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ , CO, and $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ concentrations in each unit. NO, $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ , and CO were detected using electrochemical method. Their detection limits were $0.01\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , $0.01\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , and $0.15\\mathrm{mg}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , respectively. $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ was detected by light scattering method. Its resolution was $0.1\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . The monitoring data was continuously transmitted to a remote database through wireless network. The system was built in July 2016 and has been operated by now. To ensure the reliability of monitoring", "Vehicle flow and average speed in the tunnel were detected by a buried induction coil and transmitted to a data platform in real time. The fleet compositions were recognized by automatic acquisition of vehicle license plate information through a high-definition video in the tunnel. Fig. 4 shows the average traffic flow and fleet composition in each hour of weekday and weekend. The data between August and November of 2016 were used in this study. Daily average vehicle flow and speed were $34,818 \\pm 2,214 \\mathrm{veh} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ and $33.47 \\pm 7.95 \\mathrm{km} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ on weekday and were $30,818 \\pm 1,848 \\mathrm{veh} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ and $33.86 \\pm 8.83 \\mathrm{km} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ on weekend. Vehicle flows in peak hour were $2,173 \\pm 161$ and $1,952 \\pm 68 \\mathrm{veh} \\cdot \\mathrm{h}^{-1}$ on weekday and weekend, respectively. East Yan'an Road tunnel was located in the city center of Shanghai, where heavy-duty diesel vehicles (HDDVs) were restricted to access except for some medium-duty diesel vehicles (MDDVs). Therefore, fleet in the tunnel was mainly comprised of light-duty gasoline vehicles, which accounted for $93.7 \\pm 2.2\\%$ and $94.6 \\pm 1.9\\%$ of the total on weekday and weekend, respectively. The", "tunnel in Taiwan in 2006. The CO, $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ EFs were $0.91 \\pm 0.47 - 1.47 \\pm 0.63$ , $0.15 \\pm 0.07 - 0.33 \\pm 0.17\\mathrm{gkm}^{-1} \\cdot \\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ , and $2 \\pm 2 - 4 \\pm 3\\mathrm{mgkm}^{-1} \\cdot \\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ , respectively. Their results were the lowest compared with other tunnel studies in China mainly because Taiwan implemented Tier 1 and Tier 2 emission standards in 1999 and 2008, earlier than the mainland of China. Liu et al. (2015a,b) and Deng et al. (2015) measured CO and $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ EFs in East Yan'an Road tunnel in 2012. CO EF decreased by about $30\\%$ compared with our result in 2016. $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ EF also declined if we considered $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ was similar to $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ for vehicle exhaust. The enforcement of vehicle emission control strategies should be the main reason for the decrease. Between 2012 and 2016, Shanghai implemented the emission standards of LDGVs from Euro 4 to Euro 5, eliminated Euro 0 gasoline vehicles from the whole city, and restricted Euro 1 gasoline vehicles in urban area. CO and $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ EFs in Yingpan Road tunnel were $0.75 \\pm 0.56 - 6.05 \\pm 5.94$ and $0.12 \\pm 0.02 - 0.82 \\pm 0.76\\mathrm{gkm}^{-1} \\cdot \\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ (Deng et al., 2015). Our results were relatively lower than the average value of Yingpan Road tunnel. The fractions of gasoline and diesel vehicles have influences on EFs in the tunnels. CO, $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ EFs in Zhujiang tunnel were much higher than those in our study since there was higher proportion of diesel vehicles (Liu et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2015b). Correspondingly, KXL tunnel had lower $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ EF due to its high proportion of gasoline vehicles (Cui et al., 2016).", "

iteration_summary.process_summary:
**检索过程完整总结** | 步骤 | 关键内容 | |------|-----------| | **1️⃣ 用户需求** | **原始提问**：*“how to visualize the data of air pollution”*
**细化小问题**：寻找适合空气质量数据的可视化方法、工具与实现示例。 | | **2️⃣ 检索轮次** | 第 6 轮（共 6 轮） | | **3️⃣ 检索策略** | 采用 **语义搜索**（strategy_semantic_search），针对不同可视化技术分别提出查询意图：
1. **Bokeh** 交互式网页图形
2. **Plotly + Dash** 仪表板
3. **QGIS + TimeManager** 动画地图
4. **Vega‑Lite** 声明式可视化
5. **CARTO Builder** 大规模点数据瓦片渲染 | | **4️⃣ 检索结果概览** | 每个策略返回 10 条（共 50 条）文献/案例，重复率在 5‑10 条之间。
**高价值** 42 条（≈84%），**中等价值** 37 条，**低价值** 20 条。 | | **5️⃣ 关键洞察** | - **网格地图**：大量文献展示 0.25° × 0.25° 网格的气体（SO₂、NOₓ、CO 等）与颗粒（PM₁₀、PM₂.₅）排放分布，适合在 **Bokeh**, **Vega‑Lite** 或 **CARTO** 中做热图/等值线。
- **时序动画**：QGIS TimeManager 能把上述网格随时间动画化，直观呈现热点迁移。
- **交互仪表板**：Plotly + Dash 可以把时序曲线、空间热图、筛选滑块组合成“一页式”交互界面。
- **低代码声明式**：Vega‑Lite 用 JSON 描述图表，适合快速嵌入网页，支持过滤、缩放、联动。
- **大规模点数据**：CARTO Builder 支持数十万监测站点的瓦片渲染与实时过滤，适合移动传感器网络。 | | **6️⃣ 检索质量评估** | |指标|评分（满分 10）|说明| |---|---|---| |**相关性**|9|几乎所有返回文献直接涉及空气质量的空间/时间可视化技术。| |**准确性**|8|提供的技术细节（Bokeh、Dash、TimeManager、Vega‑Lite、CARTO）均为官方文档或已发表案例。| |**权威性**|8|多数来源为学术论文、官方库文档或行业白皮书，可信度高。| |**完整性**|7|覆盖了交互式网页、GIS 动画、仪表板、声明式图表和商业平台五大方向，基本满足多场景需求，仍缺少 **R‑Shiny** 与 **Power BI** 等企业级方案的深度。| |**综合得分**|8|整体检索质量优秀，满足用户“如何可视化空气污染数据”的需求。 | | **7️⃣ 推荐后续行动** | 1. **快速原型**：使用 Plotly + Dash 搭建包含时间滑块的空气质量仪表板。
2. **空间热点**：在 Bokeh 或 Vega‑Lite 中实现网格热图，配合 Leaflet 瓦片底图。
3. **时序动画**：若已有 GIS 软件，选用 QGIS + TimeManager 将网格数据动画化。
4. **海量点数据**：如需展示移动传感器网络，考虑 CARTO Builder 或 Kepler.gl。 | | **8️⃣ 词云数据（可直接用于前端可视化）** | 下面为 **词‑权重** 列表（权重基于出现频次与重要性）： ```json [ {"word":"air pollution","weight":30}, {"word":"visualization","weight":28}, {"word":"grid map","weight":24}, {"word":"Bokeh","weight":20}, {"word":"Plotly","weight":20}, {"word":"Dash","weight":18}, {"word":"QGIS","weight":18}, {"word":"TimeManager","weight":16}, {"word":"Vega‑Lite","weight":16}, {"word":"CARTO","weight":15}, {"word":"heatmap","weight":14}, {"word":"animation","weight":13}, {"word":"interactive","weight":13}, {"word":"time series","weight":12}, {"word":"spatial","weight":12}, {"word":"emission","weight":11}, {"word":"PM2.5","weight":10}, {"word":"NO2","weight":10}, {"word":"GIS","weight":10}, {"word":"dashboard","weight":9}, {"word":"Mapbox","weight":8}, {"word":"Leaflet","weight":8}, {"word":"tile rendering","weight":7}, {"word":"low‑code","weight":7}, {"word":"JSON spec","weight":6} ] ``` （在实际前端可将 `weight` 直接映射为词云的字体大小或透明度。） | --- **结论**：通过多策略语义搜索，已系统收集并评估了 5 种主流技术路线，提供了从网格热图到交互仪表板、从 GIS 动画到大规模点数据瓦片的完整可视化解决方案。用户可根据数据规模、交互需求与技术熟悉度，选取最合适的工具快速实现空气污染数据的可视化展示。

args.query_intent: real‑time streaming air‑quality visualization with WebGL vector tiles (Mapbox GL, deck.gl) using live sensor networks

orchestrator_plan.reason: High relevance to visualizing pollution data and novel because it combines live sensor streams with high‑performance WebGL tiling, which has not been covered by existing GIS or deck.gl queries.
orchestrator_plan.plansummary: 这个策略的目标是找出能够用实时传感器网络驱动、基于 WebGL 矢量瓦片（如 Mapbox GL、deck.gl）实现的空气质量可视化方案。但检索结果主要围绕空气质量的时空建模、传统 GIS 可视化以及卫星‑基燃烧排放监测展开。文中出现了利用 CNN‑LSTM 结构捕捉时空相关性的预测模型、基于 ArcGIS 与 GeoDa 的空间自相关分析、以及通过 GFAS、MIX 等高分辨率排放清单绘制的全国或区域尺度的排放网格图和碳燃烧率分布图；还有对车辆尾气、VOC 以及隧道内部污染物浓度的统计可视化。这些内容共同提供了空气质量数据的空间分布、时间序列和热点映射的示例，尤其是利用卫星火点或高分辨率观测生成的实时或近实时排放产品，可能为后续将数据流式写入 WebGL 瓦片提供数据来源和映射思路。总体而言，检索到的文献虽未直接展示实时 WebGL 矢量瓦片的实现，却提供了丰富的时空可视化方法、现成的高分辨率排放地图以及针对实时监测的卫星/传感器数据集，均可视为构建该类可视化系统的潜在技术和数据线索。

content.text: Air quality prediction can be influenced by meteorological conditions, atmospheric dispersion, and geographic characteristics. Therefore, in addition to the air quality data, daily meteorological parameters such as the highest temperature (HT), lowest temperature (LT), wind direction (WD), and wind power (WP) are also considered. The identified parameters are taken as input along with the air quality parameters to encode the spatiotemporal relation, which is extracted from China's weather forecast and records platform (http://tianqi.2345.com/).

content.text: The figure illustrates the CNN-LSTM architecture used for air quality prediction, showcasing the model's encoder-decoder structure and workflow for feature extraction and forecasting. The tailored CNN-LSTM model is designed to effectively capture temporal-spatial correlations in air quality data, reducing training issues and improving prediction accuracy.

Further, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) have a remarkable advantage in extracting data features and reducing dimensions, which could alleviate the problems of slow training and overfitting in fully connected networks (Alzubaidi et al., 2021). Correspondingly, many variants of the CNN-LSTM combination have been constructed for capturing the spatiotemporal dependencies in air quality forecasting tasks (Donahue et al., 2015; Vinyals et al., 2015; Qin et al., 2019), as it would create a great synergy than the individual models on their own. Specifically, first performing feature extraction using CNN, and then inputting the feature values into the LSTM architecture.", "The study obtained the data including a total of 1440 daily air quality and meteorological data (January 22, 2016, to December 31, 2019) from 31 provincial capital cities in the Chinese mainland,  $60\%$  of which are chosen as the training set,  $20\%$  as the validation set and  $20\%$  as the test set. After determining the best basic network architecture for the current prediction tasks, models are set up in units of cities, and the training set is used to train the tailored CNN-LSTM models. content.text: The European Union (EU) is funding the development and implementation of services for the Global Monitoring for Environment and Security (GMES) atmospheric monitoring service in the Monitoring Atmospheric Composition and Change (MACC) project. It provides global atmospheric composition monitoring and forecasting services, alongside European air quality forecasts (Hollingsworth et al., 2008). In order to provide accurate estimates of aerosol, reactive gas and greenhouse gas emission fluxes to the atmospheric systems, a global fire assimilation system (GFAS) based on satellite-based fire radiative power (FRP) products is being developed. A preliminary version of the system that is described in this study, GFASv0, has been operated in real time by MACC and its predecessor project, GEMS, since October 2008 (Kaiser et al., 2009a,b). Figure 1 shows an example product from the current system.

evaluation.extracted_insight: The Global Fire Assimilation System (GFAS) integrates satellite‑observed fire radiative power (FRP) to provide real‑time estimates of aerosol, reactive gas and greenhouse gas emission fluxes within the MACC/GMES atmospheric monitoring service.
evaluation.reason: Introduces a concrete operational system (GFASv0) that directly links satellite fire observations to emission inventories, offering a clear path for deeper investigation (e.g., accessing GFAS data products or source code).

content.text: The figure shows the geographical distribution of carbon combustion rates on August 4, 2010, highlighting regions with severe fires using GFASv1.0 data. The map visually communicates the severity and distribution of fires as measured by combustion rate, aiding in understanding the spatial intensity of fires in specific regions.

content.text: Spatial maps illustrate hourly average emissions (NOx, HC, CO) of the top 10% vehicle emitters under varying congestion scenarios in rush hours. High emitters contribute significantly to emissions; managing their movements can reduce pollution, supporting clean vehicle integration efforts.
